perturbation.original_text: CERES will pay all such invoices within thirty (30) days after the invoice date.
perturbation.changed_text: CERES will pay all such invoices within ninety (90) days after the invoice date.
perturbation.explanation: This modification extends the payment timeline to 90 days, which may violate prompt payment laws in certain US states if the work is performed within those states by subcontractors.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 7107 requires contractors to pay subcontractors within ten days of receiving payment themselves. While this contract is between Ceres and IGER, if IGER subcontracts some of the work to a company based in California, a 90-day payment period would be in violation of CA BPC 7107.

Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 7. Disciplinary Proceedings [7090 - 7124.6] ( Article 7 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) 7107. Abandonment without legal excuse of any construction project or operation engaged in or undertaken by the licensee as a contractor constitutes a cause for disciplinary action. (Added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment timeline to 90 days, which the perturbation explanation suggests may violate prompt payment laws in certain US states. The law explanation specifically mentions California Business and Professions Code Section 7107, which requires contractors to pay subcontractors within ten days of receiving payment. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly state the 'ten-day' requirement, the law explanation establishes that a 90-day payment period would violate CA BPC 7107, thus creating a legal contradiction if the work is subcontracted to a company based in California. The original text's 30-day payment period is less likely to violate the California law than the changed text's 90-day payment period, making the change a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving Party shall have no obligations of confidentiality for information that: can be established through written evidence to be in the possession of the receiving Party prior to the disclosure by the disclosing Party; is or becomes public knowledge through no fault of the disclosing Party; and/or is acquired from others not under an obligation of confidentiality to the disclosing Party. In addition, the Parties, pursuant to the express terms of this Agreement or any SCHEDULE or other agreement between the Parties, shall have the right to proceed to disclosures of Confidential Information (a) as required to file for intellectual property protection or registration or deregulation or approval of genetically modified organisms; (b) as required to exercise commercialization rights granted in or on the basis of this Agreement and for related marketing activities; (c) as required by laws, rules or regulation or court ordering such as, without limitation, SEC regulations and IRS regulations; or (d) in CERES' reasonable judgment for the limited purpose of (potential) investors and business partners, but only with respect to the Confidential Information defined in Article 7.1 (b) and not other Confidential Information of IGER.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving Party shall have no obligations of confidentiality for information that: can be established through written evidence to be in the possession of the receiving Party prior to the disclosure by the disclosing Party; is or becomes public knowledge through no fault of the disclosing Party; and/or is acquired from others not under an obligation of confidentiality to the disclosing Party. In addition, the Parties, pursuant to the express terms of this Agreement or any SCHEDULE or other agreement between the Parties, shall have the right to proceed to disclosures of Confidential Information as required by laws, rules or regulation or court ordering such as, without limitation, SEC regulations and IRS regulations.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the exception allowing disclosure 'as required to file for intellectual property protection' and 'to potential investors'. This contradicts the duty of candor owed to the USPTO when pursuing patent protection, potentially rendering any patents obtained unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: 37 CFR § 1.56 imposes a duty of candor and good faith toward the Patent and Trademark Office. By removing the explicit allowance to disclose confidential information when filing for intellectual property protection, this creates a situation where compliance with the contractual confidentiality terms could lead to a violation of the duty of disclosure to the USPTO, potentially invalidating any patents.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 37 CFR § 1.56 - Duty to disclose information material to patentability. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 37 CFR § 1.56 - Duty to disclose information material to patentability. CFR prev | next § 1.56 Duty to disclose information material to patentability. (a) A patent by its very nature is affected with a public interest. The public interest is best served, and the most effective patent examination occurs when, at the time an application is being examined, the Office is aware of and evaluates the teachings of all information material to patentability. Each individual associated with the filing and prosecution of a patent application has a duty of candor and good faith in dealing with the Office, which includes a duty to disclose to the Office all information known to that individual to be material to patentability as defined in this section. The duty to disclose information exists with respect to each pending claim until the claim is cancelled or withdrawn from consideration, or the application becomes abandoned. Information material to the patentability of a claim that is cancelled or withdrawn from consideration need not be submitted if the information is not material to the patentability of any claim remaining under consideration in the application. There is no duty to submit information which is not material to the patentability of any existing claim. The duty to disclose all information known to be material to patentability is deemed to be satisfied if all information known to be material to patentability of any claim issued in a patent was cited by the Office or submitted to the Office in the manner prescribed by §§ 1.97(b) -(d) and 1.98. However, no patent will be granted on an application in connection with which fraud on the Office was practiced or attempted or the duty of disclosure was violated through bad faith or intentional misconduct. The Office encourages
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the explicit allowance to disclose confidential information when filing for intellectual property protection. 37 CFR § 1.56, as stated in both snippets, imposes a duty of candor and good faith toward the Patent and Trademark Office, requiring disclosure of all information material to patentability. Removing the allowance to disclose information for IP protection places the receiving party in a situation where adhering to confidentiality obligations under the contract could lead to a violation of the duty of disclosure to the USPTO, potentially invalidating any patents. This is a direct contradiction of the duty to disclose information material to patentability, as required by 37 CFR § 1.56.

perturbation.original_text: IGER shall keep accurate and detailed records in accordance with good accounting practices of all expenses and extramural income, if any, relating to the RESEARCH PROJECTS. All such records shall be subject to inspection by an independent auditor designated by CERES and reasonably acceptable to IGER within normal business hours with at least fourteen (14) days notice. Such inspection rights shall terminate, with respect to each RESEARCH PROJECT, on the third anniversary of the expiration or termination of such RESEARCH PROJECT.
perturbation.changed_text: IGER shall keep accurate and detailed records in accordance with good accounting practices of all expenses and extramural income, if any, relating to the RESEARCH PROJECTS. All such records shall be subject to inspection by an independent auditor designated by CERES. Such inspection rights shall terminate, with respect to each RESEARCH PROJECT, on the third anniversary of the expiration or termination of such RESEARCH PROJECT.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'reasonably acceptable to IGER' clause, CERES can designate an auditor with a conflict of interest or a reputation for unfair practices, potentially violating IGER's right to a fair audit process which is implied in contracts governed by US law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (New York Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law, which governs this agreement, implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract (NY UCC § 1-304). Removing IGER's right to reasonably approve the auditor allows CERES to select an auditor that is biased or incompetent, thus violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. While NY UCC refers to sales contracts of goods, New York courts have extended the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing to contracts more generally. This is confirmed in Part 39, a guide to NYC business practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification removes IGER's right to reasonably approve the auditor, potentially allowing CERES to select a biased or incompetent auditor. This violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing as the change introduces a potential conflict of interest that could undermine the fairness of the audit process. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to UCC § 1-304, states that every contract imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. By removing IGER's approval right, the modified contract weakens this obligation by allowing CERES to potentially act in bad faith during the audit process.
